content.text: Rollout Pause and Rollback Trigger Criteria

A rollout pause halts traffic advancement at the current percentage without reverting. A rollback reverts traffic to the previous stable version. Choosing the wrong response — pausing when a rollback was required, or rolling back prematurely — both have real costs. Pausing a P0 incident wastes the minutes needed to execute rollback; rolling back on transient noise adds unnecessary blast radius to an already-stressed incident. Defining trigger criteria explicitly, before a release begins, removes ambiguity from the most time-pressured moment in an on-call engineer's shift.

The trigger criteria are a pre-agreed contract between the release team and the on-call engineer. They must be specific enough to execute without judgment ('P99 > 1.2× baseline for 5 consecutive minutes') and broad enough to cover the failure modes you have actually seen.

This sub-subtopic covers the severity taxonomy for rollout signals, metric-based and qualitative pause triggers, automatic versus manual rollback triggers, the implementation of automated trigger evaluation, and the post-action process for refining thresholds from incidents.

attrs.alt: Alert decision flowchart: P0 triggers immediate rollback; P1/P2 triggers pause with 30-minute evaluation window

content.text: Assigning a severity level to a rollout signal drives which response path executes. Severity must be pre-defined per signal type, not determined during an incident.
Customer-visible outage or data integrity risk. Error rate > 1%, or P99 latency > 3× baseline, or safety filter regression detected.
Immediate automated rollback, page on-call lead
Degraded but functional. Error rate 0.1–1%, P99 latency 1.5–3× baseline, or eval score < 95% of baseline.
Pause rollout, open investigation, on-call evaluates rollback within 30 min
Below threshold but trending toward threshold. Single metric in warning range, not yet in critical range.
Pause rollout, monitor for 1 hour, no immediate action required
Informational. Metric elevated but within expected bounds for a new version under warm-up.
No pause. Log to release notes.

Severity is not a function of how surprising the signal is. A 5% error rate during a canary is a P0 regardless of whether you expected it. Train your team to execute the response, not debate the classification.
Pause triggers halt rollout advancement at the current traffic percentage. Traffic already on the new version continues serving; the percentage does not increase until the trigger clears.
For Claude API rollouts, behavioral metrics are as important as infrastructure metrics. Latency can look fine while response quality has regressed.

# rollout-alerts.yaml — Prometheus alerting rules for canary/ring rollouts
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: claude-rollout-triggers
  namespace: claude-api
spec:
  groups:
    - name: rollout.latency
      interval: 30s
      rules:
        - alert: RolloutLatencyP1
          expr: |
            (
              histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(claude_latency_seconds_bucket{version="canary"}[5m]))
              /
              histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(claude_latency_seconds_bucket{version="stable"}[5m]))
            ) > 1.30
          for: 5m
          labels:
            severity: p1
            action: pause
          annotations:
            summary: "Canary P99 latency >30% above stable"
            runbook: "https://wiki/runbooks/rollout-pause"

        - alert: RolloutLatencyP0
          expr: |
            (
              histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(claude_latency_seconds_bucket{version="canary"}[5m]))
              /
              histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(claude_latency_seconds_bucket{version="stable"}[5m]))
            ) > 2.0
          for: 2m
          labels:
            severity: p0
            action: rollback
          annotations:
            summary: "Canary P99 latency >2x stable — immediate rollback"
            runbook: "https://wiki/runbooks/rollout-rollback"

    - name: rollout.errors
      interval: 30s
      rules:
        - alert: RolloutErrorRateP1
          expr: |
            rate(claude_http_errors_total{version="canary",status=~"5.."}[5m])
            / rate(claude_http_requests_total{version="canary"}[5m]) > 0.001
          for: 5m
          labels:
            severity: p1
            action: pause

        - alert: RolloutErrorRateP0
          expr: |
            rate(claude_http_errors_total{version="canary",status=~"5.."}[5m])
            / rate(claude_http_requests_total{version="canary"}[5m]) > 0.01
          for: 2m
          labels:
            severity: p0
            action: rollback

Automatic rollback removes human latency from P0 scenarios — the window between a P0 trigger firing and rollback executing can mean thousands of additional failed requests. Manual rollback preserves human judgment for ambiguous situations where the automated signal may be misleading.
P0 triggers: latency > 2× baseline, error rate > 1%, safety regression confirmed — automate without exception
The rollback action itself is idempotent and well-tested (kubectl argo rollouts abort runs thousands of times per year across the fleet)
The cost of a false-positive automated rollback (briefly reverting a good release) is less than the cost of human latency on a real P0
An on-call engineer is always notified, even for automated rollbacks — automation acts first, humans review

P1 and P2 signals: pause is automated; rollback decision requires human review of context (is the signal a warm-up artifact? a single-region anomaly? a downstream dependency failure not caused by the rollout?)
When the rollout is at Ring 3 (GA) and rollback would affect all customers — the blast radius of rollback is larger than the blast radius of staying the course
When the release includes a DB schema migration that cannot be rolled back without data loss
When external dependencies (vendor APIs, partner integrations) were told to expect the new version
Pausing a rollout means the current traffic percentage is frozen. For Argo Rollouts, this is a first-class operation. For custom ring deployments, it means stopping the ring-advancement workflow.

# Manual pause — engineer-initiated
kubectl argo rollouts pause claude-gateway -n claude-api

# Automated pause via controller annotation (set by alert webhook receiver)
kubectl annotate rollout claude-gateway \
  argoproj.io/paused=true \
  -n claude-api \
  --overwrite

# Check pause state
kubectl argo rollouts get rollout claude-gateway -n claude-api

# Resume after investigation
kubectl argo rollouts resume claude-gateway -n claude-api

# ring_state_manager.py — centralized pause/resume for ring advancement
import json
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host="redis", port=6379, db=0)

ROLLOUT_STATE_KEY = "rollout:state:{version}"

def pause_rollout(version: str, reason: str, ring: str):
    key = ROLLOUT_STATE_KEY.format(version=version)
    state = {
        "status": "paused",
        "paused_at_ring": ring,
        "reason": reason,
        "paused_at": int(time.time()),
    }
    r.set(key, json.dumps(state))
    # Any ring-advancement workflow checks this key before proceeding
    print(f"Rollout {version} paused at {ring}: {reason}")

def resume_rollout(version: str, engineer: str):
    key = ROLLOUT_STATE_KEY.format(version=version)
    current = json.loads(r.get(key) or "{}")
    current["status"] = "active"
    current["resumed_by"] = engineer
    current["resumed_at"] = int(time.time())
    r.set(key, json.dumps(current))
    print(f"Rollout {version} resumed by {engineer}")

def is_paused(version: str) -> bool:
    key = ROLLOUT_STATE_KEY.format(version=version)
    state = json.loads(r.get(key) or '{"status": "active"}')
    return state.get("status") == "paused"
When a rollback trigger fires, the on-call engineer executes steps in a fixed order. Deviation from this order during an incident introduces coordination errors.
Acknowledge the alert in PagerDuty within 5 minutes to stop escalation
Confirm trigger: check the dashboards that fired the alert; ensure the signal is real and not a metric pipeline artifact
Execute rollback command (or confirm it already ran via automated watchdog)
Verify rollback: watch error rate and latency dashboards drop to baseline within 60 seconds
Notify customer-facing teams: #incidents Slack channel with 'rollback executed, investigating root cause'
Open incident record: document the trigger metric value, the time of rollback, and preliminary hypothesis
Begin root cause analysis — do not re-attempt the rollout until root cause is confirmed and fixed

# Argo Rollouts rollback (canary or blue-green)
kubectl argo rollouts abort claude-gateway -n claude-api

# Verify rollback complete
kubectl argo rollouts status claude-gateway -n claude-api --timeout 120s

# Force rollback to specific revision (if abort leaves an inconsistent state)
kubectl argo rollouts undo claude-gateway -n claude-api --to-revision=2

# For ring deployments: revert ring config
kubectl apply -f infra/rings/ring-config-stable.yaml

# Verify ring revert
kubectl get configmap ring-config -n claude-api -o yaml | grep version

Every rollback — automated or manual — produces evidence about the accuracy of your trigger thresholds. After each incident, the release team runs a threshold review to decide whether the trigger fired correctly or needs adjustment.
Did the trigger fire at the right severity? (P0 rollback when P1 pause would have been sufficient = threshold too sensitive)
Was the signal transient — would it have self-resolved without intervention? (warm-up spikes, single-region blip)
Was the 'for: Nm' window (duration before firing) too short or too long?
Did any qualifying trigger fail to fire? (false negative — a real regression that should have triggered but didn't)
Was the baseline computed from a representative time window? (weekday baseline used for weekend rollout = misleading comparison)

# threshold-review.yaml — post-incident threshold update record
incident_id: INC-20240315-canary-latency
rollout_version: claude-3-7-sonnet-v2
trigger_that_fired:
  alert: RolloutLatencyP1
  metric: P99 latency ratio
  threshold: 1.30
  observed_value: 1.35
  duration_fired: 8m

retrospective_verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE
  # Signal was cold-start latency spike that self-resolved after 12 min.
  # Rollback was not necessary.

proposed_change:
  alert: RolloutLatencyP1
  threshold_old: 1.30
  threshold_new: 1.40   # give 12-min warm-up room
  for_old: 5m
  for_new: 10m           # require sustained elevation before firing
  rationale: >
    Cold-start latency at 1% canary cohort causes transient P99 spikes during
    first 8-10 minutes of traffic. Raising threshold and duration prevents
    false rollbacks while still catching real regressions that persist.

approved_by: [alice@, bob@]
effective_from: "2024-03-16"

# threshold_drift.py — warn when baselines shift enough to make thresholds stale
from prometheus_api_client import PrometheusConnect
import yaml

prom = PrometheusConnect(url="http://prometheus:9090")

def check_threshold_drift(config_file: str):
    with open(config_file) as f:
        thresholds = yaml.safe_load(f)

    for metric in thresholds["metrics"]:
        current_baseline = query_baseline(prom, metric["query"], window="7d")
        recorded_baseline = metric["baseline"]
        drift_pct = abs(current_baseline - recorded_baseline) / recorded_baseline * 100

        if drift_pct > 15:
            print(f"DRIFT [{metric['name']}]: baseline drifted {drift_pct:.1f}% "
                  f"(recorded {recorded_baseline:.3f}, current {current_baseline:.3f}). "
                  f"Update rollout-alerts.yaml thresholds before next release.")

def query_baseline(prom, query: str, window: str) -> float:
    result = prom.custom_query(f"avg_over_time(({query})[{window}])")
    return float(result[0]["value"][1]) if result else 0.0

check_threshold_drift("infra/rollout-thresholds.yaml")
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Using absolute thresholds instead of baseline-relative ratios
Thresholds become stale as traffic grows; false positives or missed regressions
Always define thresholds as multiples of a rolling baseline
Cold-start and warm-up spikes trigger unnecessary pauses
Use 'for: 5m' for P1; 'for: 2m' for P0 (fast enough, not too fast)
No quality/safety triggers — only infrastructure metrics
Model behavioral regression ships through because latency and error rate look fine
Include eval score, refusal rate, and format adherence in trigger criteria
Rollback executed before alert is acknowledged
On-call doesn't know rollback is in flight; double-action risk
Automated rollback must create PD incident before executing; watchdog waits for ACK on P1
False positives accumulate; engineers stop trusting alerts; alert fatigue sets in
Mandatory threshold review within 48h of any rollback
Single region metrics used for global rollout gates
Regional anomaly (e.g., eu-west-1 CDN issue) pauses global rollout unnecessarily
Evaluate gates on 3+ region aggregate; regional alerts are informational only

Rollout pause and rollback triggers require explicit, pre-agreed criteria mapped to severity levels before a release begins. P0 triggers execute automated rollback within 2–5 minutes; P1 and P2 triggers pause advancement and give engineers 30–60 minutes to assess. Metric-based triggers must be baseline-relative, not absolute, to remain valid as traffic grows. Quality and safety signals (eval scores, refusal rates, format compliance) are as critical as infrastructure metrics for Claude API releases. After every rollback, update the threshold configuration to reduce false positives without compromising true recall.
P0: immediate automated rollback; P1: automated pause, human decides rollback within 30 min
Latency triggers use ratios (P99 > 1.3× baseline), not absolute millisecond thresholds
Claude-specific triggers: eval score < 97% of baseline, refusal rate delta > 5%, safety regression detected
Argo Rollouts implements pause/abort natively; ring deployments use a centralized state key
Post-rollback threshold review within 48h is mandatory — false positives and false negatives are both failure modes
